text: The surgeon mobilizes the esophagus along the infra azygos posterior cut using a bipolar scissor on the left arm and a monopolar cutting scissor on the right arm. The thin layer of pleura is carefully dissected, with vessels being coagulated. Attention is given to avoid thoracic duct injury during the procedure.

The surgeon performs an anterior dissection along the esophagus, carefully avoiding direct manipulation to prevent damage. Monopolar scissors are used for precise dissection, mobilizing the esophagus along its anterior border. The prone position facilitates visualization of the pulmonary vessels, left bronchus, carina, and right bronchus, while ensuring the trachea and bronchus remain uninjured. The hiatus remains unopened during this stage.

The surgeon performs a supra-azygous cut on the posterior side, followed by mobilization on the neck side and an anterior cut using bipolar and monopolar cautery. The azygous vein is clamped with Hemlock clips and cut to facilitate nodal dissection, while carefully avoiding injury to the trachea during esophagus mobilization.

The surgeon coagulates and cuts the bronchial artery, while mobilizing the esophagus from the suprasegos and trasegos regions. The esophagus is carefully separated from the liver on the opposite side, with visible slow resection. The thoracic duct is avoided to prevent injury. The dissection exposes the bronchus, carina, and bronchus, with minimal use of cautery to prevent conduction current damage. The bent monopolar scissor is utilized for precise dissection in robotic surgery.

The surgeon uses a small burst of water to assist in dissecting the esophagus, which is brought out to the area of the outer pulmonary window near the left bronchus. The left recurrent nerve is dissected, and the hiatus side is fully dissected, though not opened. The right-angled monocular scissors are utilized to identify the vagus nerve, showcasing the precision of robotic surgery.

The surgeon carefully dissects the right bronchial and parabronchial nodes, identifying the arch of the right bronchial nodes and the vagus nerve. The right recurrent laryngeal nerve is exposed, and its nodes are meticulously dissected with minimal use of cautery to prevent conduction damage. The dissected nodes are placed in a plastic pouch to prevent contamination and spillage within the thoracic cavity.

The surgeon meticulously dissects the right Arlan nodes, removing lymph nodes up to the neck side. Subcranial nodal dissection is performed with care to avoid proximity to the carina, using bipolar cautery for elevation. The left bronchial nodes are dissected and removed, followed by dissection of nodes in the left recurrent arrangia from the outer panel window.

The patient is positioned prone, and the left recurrent laryngeal nerve is visible with nodal tissue dissected and placed into a plastic pouch. The dissection continues up to the neck side, avoiding electrocautery, and proceeds to the outer pulmonary window where nodes are removed using the hump of the scissor. The patient is then turned to a supine position.

The surgeon uses bipolar cautery to immobilize the greater and lesser omentum. The 8A and 8B lymph nodes are dissected out with cautery. The third arm retracts the stomach upwards, allowing dissection of nodes over the splenic artery. All lymph nodes are removed and directed towards the specimen near the left gastric artery.

The surgeon coagulates and cuts the left gastric vein, then dissects the left gastric lymph nodes using robotic scissors and bipolar instruments. The left gastric artery is carefully dissected, and the stomach is cleared following oncological principles. An automated stapler is used to create a gastric conduit, lifting the specimen up. The patient's neck is immobilized, and the gastric conduit is prepared for cervical anastomosis.
